content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · · Administration · · · Executive/Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660             322-5901            322-6441            322-6444

June 23, 1980


Bart J. Schenone
Haley, Schenone, Birchfield
& Smith
P.O. Box 450
Hayward, CA  94543

Dear Mr. Schenone:

     Your letter requesting advice about how to report your
firm's legal services to Manuel S. Garcia has been referred to
me for reply.  On April 8, 1980, Mr. Garcia was a candidate for
the office of councilman in Union City.  He received eight fewer
votes than the third highest vote getter.  Your office provided
legal services in connection with a recount and subsequent election
contest out of which Mr. Garcia emerged as a duly elected councilman.
Your office intends to donate these services to Mr. Garcia and
you want to know whether the services you provided are to be
reported as a contribution or as a gift and how to value them
in either event.

     The Commission has addressed itself to the question of
how to report donations for legal expenses in an opinion in the
Matter of:  Douglas Buchanan, 5 FPPC Opinions 14 (No. 78-013,
May 1, 1979) (copy enclosed).  In Buchanan the Commission held
that the cost of litigation aimed at maintaining a candidate's
place on the ballot was a contribution.

     Although payments for the costs of litigation are
not generally thought of as having any connection with
political campaigns, in the circumstances presented
here and in similar circumstances, the litigation costs
are just as key to the success of the campaign as
traditional campaign costs such as mailings and media
advertisements.  When expenditures are made to support
litigation aimed at gaining a place on the ballot for a
candidate or measure, aimed at keeping a candidate or
measure off the ballot, or challenging the result of
an election, the expenditures are made for the purpose

Mr. Bart J. Schenone
June 23, 1980
Page Two


     of influencing the outcome of the election in favor
of or against a particular candidate or measure and
should be reported. Similarly, when expenditures are
made during the course of a campaign for litigation
designed to protect or vindicate the personal reputation
of a candidate, those expenditures generally are made to
forward the fortunes of the candidate in the election
and should also be reported.

                                        Buchanan, pp. 15-16

     Therefore, the cost of legal services provided Mr. Garcia should
be reported as a contribution.

     Assuming that the requirements of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18423(a)(1) and (2) are met, you are correct that the value of the
services of an employee of the firm would be reportable pursuant
to Section 18423(c). Subsection (d), however, also provides
that any other expenses incurred in connection with the services
rendered to Mr. Garcia, must also be reported by your firm as
part of the total amount contributed.

     I trust that this answers your questions. If I may be of
any further assistance to you, please do not hesitate to contact
me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Thomas M. Sobel
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

TMS:nrw
Enclosure

LAW OFFICES OF

HALEY, SCHENONE, BIRCHFIELD & SMITH

JOSEPH A. SCHENONE
J. KENNETH BIRCHFIELD
JOHN K. "JACK" SMITH

ROBERT SAKAI
BART J. SCHENONE
BERNLO G. PECK

SUITE 4 HALEY BUILDING
1331 B STREET
P.O. BOX 450
HAYWARD, CALIFORNIA 94543
TELEPHONES (415) 538-6400
(415) 351-1932

OF COUNSEL

MARLIN W. HALEY
DONALD A. PEARCE
PHILIP SILVER

FILE NO:

REPLY TO

June 11, 1980

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1100 K Street Building
Sacramento, California 95814

RE: INFORMAL ADVICE LETTER

Dear Madam or Sir:

Our law office, HALEY, SCHENONE, BIRCHFIELD & SMITH, rendered
legal services for Manuel S. Garcia, candidate for Councilman
of the City of Union City. It is the intention of this law
office to donate the services that have been rendered.

We seek an informal advice letter determining whether the
donation is a contribution or a gift under statute and regu-
lation.

On April 8, 1980, the general election of Councilman for the
City of Union City was held. Three city council positions
were to be filled. Manuel S. Garcia received eight fewer
votes than Richard P. Oliver, who received the third-highest
number of votes.

Subsequent to the April 8, 1980 election, our office was con-
tacted regarding legal services for Manuel S. Garcia for a
recount and any election contest. In fact, a recount was
conducted and an election contest was held, where Manuel S.
Garcia has been confirmed to be duly-elected.

Our office has examined the applicable Government Code, sec-
tion 82015, and the applicable regulation, section 1423, and
has determined that if the services rendered are a contribu-
tion, they must be reported as such. Subsection (b) of the
regulation defines personal services as rendered for political
purposes "if they are carried on for the purpose of influencing
or attempting to influence the action of voters." Our feeling
is that since our services occurred subsequent to the election
of April 8, 1980, it does not fall within the definition of
personal services rendered for political purposes.

Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 11, 1980
Page 2


We should like to have advice as to whether we are correct in
our assessment that the rendering of services is not a con-
tribution. If we are incorrect, then we should live further
advice as to whether, under subsection (c) of Regulation 18423,
the amount reportable is the pro-rata portion of gross salary
attributable to the time spent on political activity of the
employee of the law firm rendering such services--not the fair
market value of the services.

If we are correct in our assessment that the donation of the
personal services is a gift, please so advise. We believe
the value of the personal services as a gift would be the fair
market value of such services. Would you also so advise.

If there are any questions, please contact me. Thank you for
your cooperation and courtesy in this matter.

Respectfully,

HALEY, SCHENONE, BIRCHFIELD & SMITH

By Bart J Schenone
BART J. SCHENONE

BJS:km

sections.question: Your office provided legal services in connection with a recount and subsequent election contest for Manuel S. Garcia. Your office intends to donate these services to Mr. Garcia and you want to know whether the services you provided are to be reported as a contribution or as a gift and how to value them in either event.
sections.conclusion: Therefore, the cost of legal services provided Mr. Garcia should be reported as a contribution. Assuming that the requirements of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423(a) (1) and (2) are met, you are correct that the value of the services of an employee of the firm would be reportable pursuant to Section 18423(c). Subsection (d), however, also provides that any other expenses incurred in connection with the services rendered to Mr. Garcia, must also be reported by your firm as part of the total amount contributed.
sections.question_synthetic: Should donated legal services provided by a law firm for a candidate's recount and election contest be reported as a contribution or gift, and how should they be valued?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the donated legal services should be reported as a contribution, not a gift. The value of employee services and all other expenses incurred in connection with those services must be reported as part of the total contribution amount.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Document references the Buchanan opinion (78-013) as precedent. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not affect extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your office provided legal services in connection with a recount and subsequent election contest for Manuel S. Garcia. Your office intends to donate these services to Mr. Garcia and you want to know whether the services you provided are to be reported as a contribution or as a gift and how to value them in either event.

CONCLUSION: Therefore, the cost of legal services provided Mr. Garcia should be reported as a contribution. Assuming that the requirements of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18423(a) (1) and (2) are met, you are correct that the value of the services of an employee of the firm would be reportable pursuant to Section 18423(c). Subsection (d), however, also provides that any other expenses incurred in connection with the services rendered to Mr. Garcia, must also be reported by your firm as part of the total amount contributed.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to Bart J. Schenone's inquiry about reporting donated legal services provided to candidate Manuel S. Garcia for a recount and election contest, concluding that such services must be reported as a political contribution with the value of employee time and all related expenses included.
